description: Sage Agastya gave this prayer as a shield of light. Each of its lines hands the Sun God a different part of the body to guard, head to feet, so the chant wraps the reciter in Surya's radiance. The eight verses, preserved in the Bhavishya Purana, ask for freedom from disease, a long life, and growing physical strength and tejas, that inner glow the tradition prizes. Sunday is the day for it. Devotees also turn to it at Makar Sankranti and Ratha Saptami, when the Sun's worship peaks. About five minutes covers the whole recitation, short enough to fold into a morning routine before the day begins. Here you will find all eight verses in Devanagari, a clear Latin transliteration to guide pronunciation, the meaning in Hindi, and a PDF you can download and keep offline.

Half Shiva, half Shakti, one body, split down the middle. That is the image Adi Shankaracharya holds up across the eight verses of this hymn, where every line sets a feminine detail beside its masculine counterpart, the ash against the saffron, the serpent against the bangle. It is a meditation on the two as one, never separable. Chanting it is held to bring harmony to marriage and a meeting of complementary energies, and to grant long life, fame, and all-round prosperity, the saubhagya devotees pray for. Monday and Friday are the favoured days; Maha Shivaratri, the month of Shravan, and Navratri the favoured seasons. Roughly five minutes from start to finish. The page carries the full Devanagari text, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline reading.

Some prayers praise a deity; this one praises a city. Ayodhya, where Rama was born, becomes the object of devotion across these nine verses drawn from traditional Rama literature. Reciting it is said to carry the merit of an actual pilgrimage to Ayodhya, to draw Rama's blessings, and to open the door toward moksha, while settling peace, dharma and righteousness into daily life. Thursday and Sunday are the days devotees choose. The hymn takes on extra force at Ram Navami and at Vivah Panchami, the festival marking Rama and Sita's wedding. Set aside about eight minutes for a full reading. On this page the nine verses appear in Devanagari, alongside an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF you can keep for offline recitation.

Sarva dharman parityajya mam ekam sharanam vraja, abandon all duties and come to Me alone. That single line, Krishna's final word to Arjuna, sits inside this eighteenth chapter, and it is the verse devotees most return to. Titled Moksha Sannyasa Yoga, the chapter closes the entire Bhagavad Gita, gathering every earlier teaching into one summary and sorting knowledge, action and the actor each by the three gunas. It teaches the highest surrender there is. Drawn from the Gita within the Mahabharata, its seventy-eight verses run to about thirty-five minutes, a Wednesday reading by tradition, and a natural one for Janmashtami. The page sets out all seventy-eight verses in Devanagari with English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline study.

A thousand names for Shiva's fiercest face. The Bhairava Sahasranama Stotram, kept in the Rudra Yamala Tantra, calls on Lord Bhairava in name after name, summoning all eight of his forms at once. The tradition reaches for it when protection is needed against evil spirits and hostile forces, and recites it to lift planetary afflictions and Yama's punishments. It is heavy work, and the days suit it: Saturday and Tuesday, and above all Kala Bhairava Ashtami, the night of Bhairava's own appearance. The text runs to ten verses, roughly seven minutes spoken aloud, manageable enough for steady practice. On this page you will find the complete Devanagari, an English transliteration to follow the sound, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Hymns to Brahma are scarce, which makes this one worth knowing. The Creator is rarely worshipped directly, yet here he is addressed across eight verses as Hiranyagarbha, the golden womb, as Prajapati, lord of creatures, and as Vidyadhipa, master of knowledge. The text draws on Vedic ground, carrying mantras from both the Rig Veda and the Atharvaveda, and comes down through the Brahma Purana and Vedic tradition. Reciting it is held to awaken creative power, wisdom, and knowledge of the Vedas. Thursday is its day, with Brahma Jayanti and Guru Purnima the occasions when it is read most. Four minutes is enough for the whole thing. The page gives the Devanagari, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF.

Kali facing south, the direction of death, fearless and fierce. The Dakshina Kali Stotram invokes exactly that form, and the Kalika Purana that preserves it does not soften her. Eight verses call on her protective fury, the same power that once finished Raktabija and every enemy and evil force around the devotee. Reciting it is held to draw out the deepest fears and replace them with fearlessness, and to open all the siddhis through the tantric current of Mahakali. Tuesday and Saturday carry it best; Kali Puja and Diwali are its great nights. Around seven minutes spoken in full. On this page the eight verses appear in Devanagari with an English transliteration, the meaning rendered in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Dattatreya carries Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva in a single body, the Trimurti made one, and this armour-prayer asks all three at once to stand guard. Drawn from the Narada Purana, its eight verses build a complete shield, turning back enemies, lifting disease, and pacifying graha dosha, the trouble the planets send. Recited with the right understanding and dhyana, the tradition says, it opens every siddhi. Thursday is the natural day, the day of the guru, and the chant gains weight at Guru Purnima and Datta Jayanti. A full reading takes about eight minutes, easily slotted into daily worship. The page carries all eight verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF you can download and use offline.

Of Lakshmi's many forms, Dhanalakshmi is the one who gives wealth outright, and this is her hymn. Across twelve verses it asks her to cut away debt, the line reads rinam chhindhi, and to scatter the enemies that wealth so often attracts. It prays for profit in trade and plenty in the fields, and the tradition recommends reciting it at dawn to keep debt at bay and let prosperity build steadily. Friday belongs to Lakshmi, and the hymn comes into its own at Diwali and Dhanteras, the days of her worship. The source is traditional. About eight minutes covers a full reading. On this page you will find the twelve verses in Devanagari, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Om Jai Ambe Gauri, the line that fills temple courtyards every Navratri evening. This is the most widely sung of all Durga aartis, carried in the traditional Aarti Sangrah and known in nearly every household. Its twelve verses hail Ambe Gauri and ask her protection from evil and negativity, her gift of courage and strength, and the lifting of every fear; sung with devotion, the tradition holds, it fulfils whatever the heart desires. Tuesday and Friday are its days, Navratri and Durga Puja its great festivals, when the lamp circles the image and voices rise together. About six minutes to sing through. The page sets out all twelve verses in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline use.

Thirty-two names, every one of them opening with Durga, strung together into a single namavali. This Durga Dvatrimshan Namavali comes from the Durga Saptashati of the Markandeya Purana, and each name carries a distinct protective power of the Goddess. Tradition turns to it against fear, enemies, fevers and disease, and advises reciting it at all three sandhya, the junctures of dawn, noon and dusk, for protection that holds through the day. Eight verses, only about three minutes spoken, which is part of why it is recited so often. Tuesday and Friday are its days; Navratri and Durga Ashtami its peak occasions. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Before an exam, a wedding, any task that matters, this is the hymn many recite first. The Ganesh Dwadash Nama Stotram, kept in the Mudgala Purana, actually holds two sets of twelve sacred names of Ganesha, a double measure meant for the fullest blessing. Calling them out is said to clear obstacles, grant knowledge, fame and a happy home, and, recited in the morning, to turn the whole world favourable toward the devotee. Wednesday is Ganesha's day, and the stotram is read most at Ganesh Chaturthi and on the monthly Sankashti Chaturthi. Seven verses, about four minutes aloud. The page gives the complete text in Devanagari with English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Garuda, the great eagle who carries Vishnu and feeds on serpents, is the whole point of this prayer, written in the long galloping dandaka metre that gives it its name. Its ten verses, from the Garuda Purana, are recited against the fear of snakes and sarpa dosha, that affliction of the serpents, and they are held to neutralise every kind of poison and venom, to lift disease and ailment, and to keep enemies and evil spirits at a distance. Sunday and Saturday are the days for it. Garuda Panchami and Nag Panchami, both tied to serpents, are its festivals. About seven minutes to recite in full. On this page you will find the Devanagari text, an English transliteration, the meaning in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF for offline use.

Jayadeva's Gita Govinda opens by counting Vishnu's ten descents into the world, and this Dashavatara Ashtapadi is that opening, the most celebrated of all such hymns in Indian literature. Across ten verses it names the avatars one by one, from the fish to the future Kalki, and remembering them is held to draw their protection. Devotees recite it to purify the heart, taste a measure of devotional ecstasy, and move toward release from the round of birth and death. Thursday and Wednesday are its days; Janmashtami and Ekadashi its occasions. Six minutes or so for a full reading. The page presents all ten verses in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to follow the melody, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

When Indra sent his storm, Krishna lifted Govardhan Hill on one finger and held it like an umbrella over Gokul until the rain gave up. This stotram, sung at Govardhan Puja on Kartik Shukla Pratipada, celebrates exactly that rescue. Its ten verses, rooted in the Bhagavata Purana and the Govardhan Puja tradition, ask for go-sampad, the wealth of cattle, and dhana, material prosperity, and for the hill's own grace to clear away obstacles and fill the home with grain and abundance. Wednesday is its day; Govardhan Puja and Annakut, when mountains of food are offered, are its festivals. About seven minutes to recite. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline reading.

Sankat Mochan, the remover of crises, is how Tulsidas frames Hanuman in this eight-stanza prayer. Each verse recalls a moment when Hanuman lifted someone out of trouble, building the case that he will do the same again for whoever recites it. Devotees turn to it against every kind of hardship, for protection from negative forces, for courage and fearlessness, and for the fulfilment of righteous wishes; the tradition even credits it with curing chronic and stubborn disease. Tuesday and Saturday are Hanuman's days, and Hanuman Jayanti his great festival. Though only eight verses, a measured recitation runs to about twelve minutes. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

The Parasara Samhita keeps some of the most potent Hanuman material in the tradition, and this stuti is one of its gems. Eleven verses of praise that, recited daily, are said to bring the siddhi of every desire. Devotees lean on it for success in whatever they undertake through Hanuman's grace, for courage, strength and protection, and against disease, ill health, and the press of negative energies, ghosts and evil spirits. Tuesday and Saturday are the days to recite it; Hanuman Jayanti the festival when it carries most force. A full reading takes around ten minutes. On this page you will find the eleven verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration to guide the sound, the meaning in Hindi, and a PDF you can download and keep offline.

Three short verses, the gayatri form, addressed to the lord of Puri. The Jagannath Gayatri, drawn from the Skanda Purana, is a Vedic meditation mantra that calls down Jagannath's supreme blessing in a handful of lines. Reciting it is held to wash away sin and purify the one who chants, to carry prosperity, happiness and grace into life, and ultimately to open toward moksha and spiritual insight. Thursday is its day. It gathers special meaning at Rath Yatra, when Jagannath rolls through the streets on his great chariot, and at Snana Yatra, his ceremonial bathing. Barely two minutes to recite, ideal for daily japa. On this page the three verses appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline use.

Kabir wrote in the language of weavers and water-carriers, and his couplets have outlasted the kings of his time. This selection gathers fifteen key dohe from the Kabir Granthavali, plain Hindi verses that turn ordinary moments, a word spoken in anger, an hour wasted, a quarrel with a neighbour, into lessons on humility, compassion and love of God. They press guru bhakti and inner change above all, offering wisdom anyone can carry, whatever their background. There are no fixed days for these; Guru Purnima, the day of the teacher, is when they ring truest. About eight minutes to read through the fifteen. The page presents the verses in Devanagari with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline reading.

At Kanchipuram sits Kamakshi, the goddess of the loving eye, and this stotram from the Markandeya Purana sings to her in ten verses. It carries the weight of the Kamakoti Peetham lineage behind it, and devotees recite it for the fulfilment of desires through Kamakshi's grace, for saubhagya, the bliss and good fortune of married life, and for the threefold gift of knowledge, wealth and progeny. Friday and Tuesday are the days to chant it; Navratri and Lalita Panchami the festivals when her worship gathers. A full recitation runs to about seven minutes. On this page you will find the ten verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration to follow the sound, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Ketu has no head, only a body, the severed tail of the cosmic serpent, and he rules the shadows of the horoscope. This short stotra from the Skanda Purana addresses that shadow planet directly. Its eight verses are recited to soften Ketu's afflictions, to carry a soul through the long stretch of a Ketu mahadasha or its shorter antardasha, and to handle the strange, chronic illnesses the tradition lays at his door. There is a gift on the far side too: spiritual knowledge, detachment, and the pull toward moksha that Ketu, oddly, also grants. Tuesday and Saturday are his days. About five minutes to recite the eight verses. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline use.

This armour is built from Krishna's own names, each one set over a part of the body to guard it. Twelve verses from the Brahmavaivarta Purana arrange the protection by direction as well, calling on the Chaturvyuha, Sankarshana, Pradyumna and Aniruddha, to hold the quarters of space. Reciting it is said to lift sin, disease, sorrow and enemies, and to confer sarva-mangala, blessing on every side. The tradition treats it as preliminary knowledge: one should know this kavacha before chanting Krishna's mantras for siddhi. Wednesday and Thursday are its days; Janmashtami and Ekadashi its occasions. Around seven minutes to recite. On this page the twelve verses appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Twelve names of Lakshmi, no more, and the Padma Purana promises they are enough. Recited at dawn, this short stotram is said to summon whatever wealth the devotee desires, to push back poverty and money troubles, and to draw down auspiciousness and a lift in everyday happiness. The whole point of the namavali is concentration: each name a small invocation, the twelve together calling the goddess close. Friday is Lakshmi's day, and the verses carry most weight at Diwali and during the Varalakshmi Vrata. Only five verses, about five minutes spoken aloud, which suits both daily worship and the festival altar. The page presents the text in Devanagari with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF you can download and keep offline.

Lalita Tripurasundari, the supreme Devi of the Sri Vidya tradition, is praised here under a full thousand names, each one a window onto her. This sahasranama comes from the Lalitopakhyana of the Brahmanda Purana, and the tradition holds nothing back about what it gives: all four purusharthas, dharma, artha, kama and moksha; beauty, grace and a magnetic presence settling on the reciter; and the burning away of sins, doshas and karmic debts piled up across lifetimes. Recited fully it leads to union with the Divine Mother herself. Tuesday and Friday are its days, Navratri and Lalita Jayanti its festivals. Arranged here in fifty verse-units, it takes about twenty-five minutes. The page carries the complete Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

The buffalo-demon Mahishasura believed no man and no god could kill him; he had not reckoned on the Goddess. This stotram, attributed to Adi Shankaracharya, sings of the one who slew him, and its sixteen tumbling verses are among the most powerful summonings of divine feminine energy in the tradition. Reciting it is held to clear away demonic and negative forces, to dissolve fear, anxiety and every form of suffering, and to confer divine beauty, grace and spiritual radiance. It pairs naturally with courage and victory over enemies. Tuesday and Friday are its days; Navratri its season. The rhythm carries a full reading to about fifteen minutes. On this page you will find all sixteen verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF.

Dakshinamurthy is Shiva as the silent teacher, seated facing south, instructing through stillness, and this stotram joins his worship to the Vedic Medha Suktam for a single end: sharper intellect. Its eight verses pray for medha, brilliance of mind and a retentive memory, and for prajna, the deeper transcendental wisdom, drawing on the older Suktam's invocation of Varuna, Agni, Indra, Vayu and Savita for intelligence. The closing verse asks for doubts and ignorance to be cleared away outright. Thursday, the guru's day, suits it; Vasant Panchami and Guru Purnima are its festivals. About six minutes to recite. The page carries the eight verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the meaning in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download for offline study.

Narada set out to define love of God in the fewest words possible, and the Narada Bhakti Sutra is the result. This selection gathers seventeen of its essential sutras, the lines where he calls pure bhakti the supreme love for the divine, teaches a devotion stripped of desire and built on complete surrender, and ranks that path above both jnana and karma. They are terse, almost epigrammatic, and meant to be turned over slowly. No fixed days govern them; Guru Purnima, the teacher's day, is the natural occasion. About eight minutes to read through the seventeen. On this page the verses appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline reading.

Sage Vyasa composed these nine verses, one for each of the nine planets, and the tradition says they are guaranteed to bring peace. The Navagraha Stotra, kept in the Skanda Purana, addresses the grahas in turn and asks each to lift the affliction it casts. Reciting it is held to clear away the trouble that comes from thieves, from fire, and from the planetary doshas themselves, to break the grip of bad dreams and nightmares, and to draw in incomparable wealth, good health and nourishment. There are no fixed days or festivals attached; it can be recited whenever the planets press hardest. About five minutes for all nine verses. The page presents the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline recitation.

Five faces, one Hanuman: the monkey, the eagle, the lion, the boar and the horse, each turned to a different quarter, each a different deity's force. This kavacham from the Parasara Samhita invokes that fivefold form, and its five verses raise a guard of protection and auspiciousness around the devotee. Reciting it is said to call down Hanuman's blessings in every form at once, to lift sin, and to lay up spiritual merit. Tuesday and Saturday are Hanuman's days; Hanuman Jayanti the festival when the chant carries furthest. Short and dense, the five verses run to about three minutes, easy to keep in daily practice. On this page you will find the full text in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline use.

Twilight on the thirteenth tithi, the hour called Pradosha, is when Shiva is said to be most easily pleased, and this is the story read during the vrat that observes it. Drawn from the Shiva Purana, its fifteen verses carry the tale of a Brahmana couple whose childlessness ends through Shiva's grace, the kernel of why the fast is kept. The katha also notes that the day of the week shapes the gift: Som Pradosha brings mental peace, Bhaum Pradosha health, Shani Pradosha children, and Pradosha on different days lifts different planetary afflictions. Maha Shivaratri and the Pradosh observance are its festivals. About ten minutes to read aloud. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline use.

Eight verses, each one a benediction, sung to Shri Radha at the start of the day. The Radha Mangalashtakam, from the Brahmavaivarta Purana, is built as an ashtakam of auspiciousness: every stanza calls down mangala, blessing, until the full sarva-mangala, well-being on every side, settles over the reciter. Devotees take it up for divine love and prema-bhakti, that pure devotional longing Radha embodies, and it is held to be the ideal morning recitation for her followers. Wednesday and Friday are its days; Radha Ashtami, Holi and Sharad Purnima its festivals. Only eight verses, about three minutes spoken, light enough for every dawn. On this page the text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

This kavacham fits Rama's divine names over the body like plate armour, each name set to guard a particular limb, the head, the eyes, the chest, on down. Drawn from traditional Vaishnavite literature, its twelve verses extend the protection past the body to every realm the tradition knows, patala below, bhutala the earth, yamala, and the open sky. Reciting it is held to grant longevity, happiness, sons, victory, and humility, the last named as squarely as the rest. Thursday and Saturday are its days; Ram Navami and Dussehra its festivals, the days of Rama's birth and his triumph. About seven minutes to recite the twelve verses. On this page you will find the full Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline use.

When the lamps at Shirdi are lowered for the night, the Shej Aarti is sung, the last of the day's four aartis, the one that puts Sai Baba to rest. Carried in the Shirdi Sai Baba Sansthan tradition, its three verses settle a quiet over the shrine and over the devotee, asking Baba's protection through the dark hours and a sleep without unrest. Handing the day's worries to the Guru before sleep, the tradition holds, dissolves them, and the act deepens the bond between teacher and disciple, the surrender at the heart of Sai devotion. Thursday, the Guru's day, suits it, as does Guru Purnima. About five minutes to sing. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline use.

Twelve names of Saraswati, and the promise behind them is striking: that the goddess of speech comes to rest on the very tongue of the one who recites them. This dwadash nama stotram, traditional in source, gathers her twelve sacred names into a short invocation said to grant mastery across every branch of vidya, every form of knowledge, and to make the reciter respected and honoured in all the worlds. Wednesday and Thursday are its days; Vasant Panchami, the spring festival of learning, is its great occasion. Seven verses, about five minutes spoken, brief enough for a student's daily habit. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to follow the sound, the meaning in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

A horoscope marked by sarpa dosha, the serpent's curse, is what this stotram sets out to undo. Drawn from the Naga Purana, its eight verses call on all nine Nagas and on Manasa Devi, the serpent goddess, to stand between the devotee and the affliction. Recited with devotion, it is held to neutralise even Kala Sarpa Dosha, the heaviest of these patterns, to pacify the troubles of Rahu and Ketu, and to lift the fear of snakes and the misfortunes the tradition ties to them. Saturday and Tuesday are its days; Naga Panchami, the festival of serpents, its great occasion. About four minutes to recite. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline use.

Hridayam means heart, and this is the heart-essence of Shani, the slow and stern planet, distilled into eight verses from the Skanda Purana. Devotees reach for it under the long shadow of Sade Sati and the weight of karma dosha, reciting it to ease every Shani-related affliction and to clear away the poverty, disease and enemy-troubles the tradition lays at Saturn's door. It is meant to bring the planet's peace rather than to fight him. Saturday, his own day, is when it is chanted; Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya are its occasions. Eight verses, about four minutes aloud, short enough for weekly observance. On this page you will find the full text in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Om, peace, peace, peace, the threefold benediction that opens and closes every spiritual undertaking in the tradition. This Shanti Path, rooted in the Yajurveda at 36.17 and in the Upanishads, is the Vedic prayer recited before and after practice to settle peace over the surroundings and within the one who chants. Its seven verses ask relief from the three sufferings the tradition names, adhyatmika from oneself, adhibhautika from other beings, adhidaivika from forces beyond, and call down physical health, mental quiet and spiritual tranquillity. No fixed days or festivals bind it; it belongs to every beginning and end. About five minutes to recite. The page carries the Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline use.

Forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, chalis being forty, and framing dohas round the recitation out to forty-three verse-units in all. A traditional Hindi devotional composition, the Shiv Chalisa is among the most recited of all Shiva texts, simple enough to learn by heart yet asked to do a great deal. Chanting it is held to fulfil the devotee's wishes, to clear obstacles, sorrows and fears, to draw wealth, prosperity and comfort, and to ward off enemies and evil forces. Monday is Shiva's day; Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan are its seasons. Counted as forty verses, it runs to about twelve minutes aloud. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

One hundred and eight names of Mahadeva, told like beads on a mala, one name to each turn. This Shiva Ashtottara Shatanamavali from the Shiva Purana is the namavali poured over the linga during Shiva Abhishek and Rudrabhishek, each name an offering as the water or milk runs down. Reciting the hundred and eight is held to draw the complete grace of Mahadeva, to lift sins, sorrows and obstacles from life, and to open toward moksha and release from the round of birth and death. Monday is Shiva's day; Maha Shivaratri, Pradosh and the month of Shravan its great seasons. The full namavali takes about fifteen minutes. The page carries all 108 names in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline use.

Shankaracharya's Shivananda Lahari runs to a hundred verses; this is a chosen handful of five, a doorway into the larger flood of devotion he poured out to Shiva. Even excerpted, the lines carry the work's purpose: to invoke Shiva's blessings in all his forms, to draw down divine protection and auspiciousness, and to lift sin while laying up spiritual merit. They reward slow, attentive recitation rather than haste. Monday, Shiva's day, suits them, and Maha Shivaratri is the festival when they are read most. Five verses, about four minutes spoken, light enough to fold into daily worship. On this page the selection appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to guide the sound, the meaning in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download for offline reading.

Keep the Friday fast, hear this story, and Lakshmi takes up permanent residence in your home, that is the promise of the Shukravar Vrat Katha. Drawn from the Lakshmi Purana, its twelve verses are read by those observing the Friday vrat for the goddess, with one rule the katha is firm about: no sour food while the fast holds, or the effect is lost. Reciting it is held to bring wealth, prosperity, children and good fortune, and to settle the afflictions of Shukra, the planet Venus. Friday is its day; Diwali and Dhanteras its festivals. About ten minutes to read aloud. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

Somnath is the first of the twelve Jyotirlingas, and this stotram from the Shiva Purana treats its recitation as the equal of standing before the shrine itself, a pilgrimage made in words. Its ten verses are held to burn away sins gathered across many lifetimes, to lift the effects of curses and planetary afflictions, and to carry the devotee toward liberation and union with Shiva. The hymn keeps the temple's gravity even when read far from the Gujarat coast. Monday is Shiva's day; Maha Shivaratri and the Shravan Somvar Mondays are its great occasions. About seven minutes to recite the ten verses. On this page you will find the full Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline use.

Like its companion armour-prayers, the Surya Kavacham covers the body limb by limb, but here it is the Sun God who stands guard at every point. Drawn from the Bhavishya Purana, its nine verses are recited against disease, the tradition singling out ailments of the eyes and conditions of the skin, the domains the Sun is said to rule. Reciting it is held to wash away sin, to set auspiciousness over every undertaking, and to pacify planetary afflictions, especially the doshas tied to Surya himself. Sunday is its day; Makar Sankranti and Ratha Saptami, the Sun's festivals, its peak occasions. About eight minutes for a full reading. The page carries the nine verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF for offline recitation.

Andal, the only woman among the twelve Alvars, sang the Thiruppavai as a girl in love with Krishna, calling her friends to rise before dawn through the cold Margazhi month and go to him. Its thirty verses, part of the Nalayira Divya Prabandham, sit at the centre of the Shri Vaishnava tradition, recited daily through that month at temples across the south. The poems carry the grace of Krishna and of Sri Ranganatha, and reciting them each day through Margazhi, the Margashirsha month, is held to fulfil the devotee's desires. Daily recitation is the practice; Margashirsha and Vaikuntha Ekadashi its festivals. About fifteen minutes for the thirty. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline use.

Vairagya Sandipani means the kindling of dispassion, and that is precisely what Tulsidas set out to light in these twelve verses. The hymn works on attachment directly, loosening the hold of worldly pleasures and giving the reciter wisdom about how transient the world really is. It is built to help overcome the inner enemies, lust, anger, greed and pride, while turning devotion ever more firmly toward Rama. Sunday and Saturday are its days; Ram Navami its festival. About eight minutes to read through. The point is reflection as much as recitation. On this page you will find the twelve verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration to follow the sound, the meaning rendered in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline reading.

Vayu is wind and Vayu is breath, the prana that moves in every living body, and this gayatri from the Rig Veda calls on him in three short verses. As a Vedic meditation mantra it is recited to strengthen the life force, the prana, within the body, to draw down vitality, speed and agility, and to clear and purify the surrounding air. The tradition also ties it to respiratory health, a fitting concern for the god of breath. No fixed days or festivals attach to it; it can be taken up whenever breath and energy need steadying. Barely two minutes for all three verses. On this page the text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Panjara means cage, and this stotram builds one of diamond around the devotee, a hard bright enclosure that nothing harmful can enter. Drawn from the Narada Purana, its eleven verses raise protection on all ten directions, scatter enemies, and grant victory in whatever the reciter undertakes. The chant is also held to lift sin, set auspiciousness in its place, and turn back disease and ailment. The image is the whole point: not a shield held in front, but walls on every side. Thursday and Sunday are its days; Ekadashi its festival. About eight minutes to recite the eleven verses. On this page you will find the full Devanagari, an English transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline use.
